# Pairing Matrix — Dish Categories to Wine Attributes

Structured lookup for translating dish types into target wine profiles. Each category lists ideal grape varieties, body, and key principles. Grapes listed are those **present in the cellar**.

---

## Red Meat

### Grilled Steak / Ribeye / T-Bone
- **Weight:** Heavy
- **Wine:** Full-bodied red with firm tannin
- **Target grapes:** Cabernet Sauvignon, Malbec, Syrah, Petit Verdot, Lagrein
- **Regions:** Bordeaux, Mendoza, Vallée du Rhône, Western Cape
- **Principle:** Tannin binds with protein; fat coats palate; char matches oak
- **Avoid:** Light whites, Pinot Noir (too delicate), sweet wines

### Braised / Slow-Cooked (lamb shanks, osso buco, beef bourguignon, pot roast)
- **Weight:** Heavy, rich sauce
- **Wine:** Full-bodied red with moderate tannin and good acid
- **Target grapes:** Merlot, Cabernet Franc, Mourvèdre, Grenache, Tempranillo, Syrah
- **Regions:** Bordeaux (Right Bank), Vallée du Rhône, La Rioja
- **Principle:** Long-cooked dishes develop complex flavours; match with complex aged reds. Acid cuts through richness.
- **Avoid:** Very tannic young wines (too austere); light wines (overwhelmed)

### Roast Lamb (rack, leg, shoulder)
- **Weight:** Medium-Heavy
- **Wine:** Medium to full red; herbal notes welcome
- **Target grapes:** Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, Cabernet Franc, Syrah, Tempranillo
- **Regions:** Bordeaux, La Rioja, Vallée du Rhône
- **Principle:** Lamb's slight gaminess pairs with herbal/earthy wines. Rosemary/thyme in the dish bridges to Cabernet Franc and Syrah aromatics.
- **Avoid:** Delicate whites, very fruity reds

### Game (venison, wild boar, duck breast)
- **Weight:** Heavy, intense flavour
- **Wine:** Full-bodied red with earthy complexity
- **Target grapes:** Nebbiolo, Syrah, Mourvèdre, Pinot Noir (mature), Cabernet Sauvignon
- **Regions:** Piemonte (Barolo/Barbaresco), Vallée du Rhône, Bordeaux
- **Principle:** Game's intensity needs wines with structure and earthy depth. Aged Nebbiolo is the classic.
- **Avoid:** Simple fruity reds, most whites

### Hamburgers / Meatballs / Casual Red Meat
- **Weight:** Medium
- **Wine:** Medium red, fruit-forward
- **Target grapes:** Merlot, Primitivo, Malbec, Grenache, Barbera, Tempranillo
- **Regions:** Mendoza, Puglia, La Rioja, Castilla y León
- **Principle:** Casual food → approachable wine. Don't open the Montrose for a burger.
- **Price tier:** Budget or everyday preferred

---

## Poultry

### Roast Chicken (simple, herbs)
- **Weight:** Medium
- **Wine:** Medium white or light-medium red
- **Target grapes (white):** Chardonnay, Chenin Blanc, Sauvignon Blanc
- **Target grapes (red):** Pinot Noir, Barbera, Merlot (lighter)
- **Regions:** Bourgogne, Western Cape, Piemonte
- **Principle:** Versatile pairing — depends on preparation. Herb roast → white with herbal notes. Rich gravy → light red.

### Chicken in Cream Sauce (Geschnetzeltes, chicken Alfredo)
- **Weight:** Medium-Heavy (cream)
- **Wine:** White with good acid or oaked richness
- **Target grapes:** Chardonnay (oaked), Chenin Blanc, Sémillon
- **Regions:** Bourgogne, Western Cape
- **Principle:** Cream needs acid to balance. Oaked Chardonnay mirrors butter richness.
- **Avoid:** Tannic reds (clash with cream), very light wines

### Grilled / BBQ Chicken
- **Weight:** Medium
- **Wine:** Rosé, fruity white, or light red
- **Target grapes:** Rosé blends, Grenache, Cinsault, Sauvignon Blanc, Chenin Blanc
- **Regions:** Vallée du Rhône, Western Cape
- **Principle:** BBQ char → smoky wines work. Rosé bridges red-meat marinades and lighter protein.

### Duck (confit, roast, magret)
- **Weight:** Heavy (fatty)
- **Wine:** Medium-full red with acid to cut fat
- **Target grapes:** Pinot Noir (Bourgogne style), Nebbiolo, Sangiovese, Barbera
- **Regions:** Bourgogne, Piemonte
- **Principle:** Duck fat demands acidity. Classic Burgundy pairing. Fruity Pinot Noir mirrors cherry/plum sauces.

---

## Fish & Seafood

### White Fish (sole, sea bass, cod, halibut — grilled or baked)
- **Weight:** Light
- **Wine:** Light-bodied, crisp white
- **Target grapes:** Sauvignon Blanc, Chasselas, Arneis, Nascetta, Pinot Gris
- **Regions:** Marlborough, Vaud, Piemonte
- **Principle:** Delicate fish needs delicate wine. High acid enhances lemon-squeeze effect.
- **Avoid:** Any tannic red (metallic bitterness), oaky whites

### Oily Fish (salmon, tuna, swordfish — grilled or seared)
- **Weight:** Medium
- **Wine:** Medium white or light red
- **Target grapes (white):** Chardonnay (unoaked or lightly oaked), Chenin Blanc, Sémillon
- **Target grapes (red):** Pinot Noir (light), Barbera
- **Regions:** Bourgogne, Western Cape, Piemonte
- **Principle:** Oily fish can handle more body. Grilled salmon + Pinot Noir is a modern classic.

### Shellfish (shrimp, crab, lobster, langoustine)
- **Weight:** Light-Medium
- **Wine:** Crisp white with mineral notes
- **Target grapes:** Sauvignon Blanc, Chasselas, Chenin Blanc, Chardonnay (unoaked)
- **Sparkling:** Champagne (Perrier-Jouët, Dom Pérignon in cellar)
- **Principle:** Shellfish sweetness pairs with mineral acidity. Champagne + lobster is iconic.
- **Avoid:** Tannic reds, heavily oaked whites

### Sushi / Sashimi
- **Weight:** Light, delicate
- **Wine:** Very crisp white or sparkling
- **Target grapes:** Sauvignon Blanc, Chasselas, Arneis, Riesling (dry)
- **Sparkling:** Champagne
- **Principle:** Minimal intervention wines match raw fish purity. Acid cleanses the palate between pieces.
- **Avoid:** All reds (tannin + raw fish = metallic), oaky whites

### Seafood Stew / Bouillabaisse / Mixed Seafood
- **Weight:** Medium
- **Wine:** Rosé or medium white
- **Target grapes:** Rosé blends, Sauvignon Blanc, Sémillon, Grenache (rosé)
- **Regions:** Vallée du Rhône (rosé), Bordeaux blanc
- **Principle:** Mixed seafood needs a versatile wine. Provençal rosé with bouillabaisse is the classic.

---

## Pasta & Rice

### Tomato-Based Pasta (marinara, arrabbiata, ragu, Bolognese)
- **Weight:** Medium-Heavy (depending on meat)
- **Wine:** Medium red with high acid
- **Target grapes:** Sangiovese, Barbera, Nebbiolo (lighter), Primitivo
- **Regions:** Toscana, Piemonte, Puglia
- **Principle:** Tomato acid needs wine acid. Sangiovese + tomato = regional perfection. Tannin balances meat ragu.

### Cream-Based Pasta (carbonara, Alfredo, cacio e pepe)
- **Weight:** Medium-Heavy (fat)
- **Wine:** White with acid, or light Italian red
- **Target grapes (white):** Chardonnay, Arneis, Nascetta
- **Target grapes (red):** Barbera (high acid cuts cream), Sangiovese
- **Regions:** Piemonte, Toscana
- **Principle:** Fat needs acid. Carbonara's egg-and-cheese richness begs for Barbera's cut.

### Pesto Pasta
- **Weight:** Medium
- **Wine:** Crisp white with herbal notes
- **Target grapes:** Arneis, Sauvignon Blanc, Nascetta, Verdejo
- **Regions:** Piemonte, Marlborough
- **Principle:** Basil's herbal intensity matches herbal wines. Pine nuts add richness → need some body.

### Risotto (mushroom, truffle, saffron)
- **Weight:** Medium-Heavy
- **Wine:** Depends on risotto type
- **Mushroom/truffle → red:** Nebbiolo, Pinot Noir (earthy notes bridge)
- **Saffron/seafood → white:** Arneis, Chardonnay, Nascetta
- **Regions:** Piemonte (Barolo with truffle risotto is peak)

---

## Asian Cuisine

### Thai (curry, pad thai, tom yum)
- **Weight:** Medium, but high heat/spice
- **Wine:** Off-dry white or aromatic white
- **Target grapes:** Gewürztraminer, Riesling (off-dry), Chenin Blanc, Sauvignon Blanc
- **Principle:** Residual sugar tames chilli heat. Aromatic wines match lemongrass/galangal.
- **Avoid:** Tannic reds (amplifies heat), high-alcohol wines

### Japanese (teriyaki, tempura, ramen)
- **Weight:** Light-Medium
- **Wine:** Crisp white, sparkling, or light red
- **Target grapes:** Riesling, Sauvignon Blanc, Pinot Noir (light)
- **Sparkling:** Champagne works with tempura
- **Principle:** Umami in soy/dashi needs acid. Teriyaki's sweetness → off-dry or fruity.

### Indian (curry, tandoori, biryani)
- **Weight:** Medium-Heavy with strong spice
- **Wine:** Aromatic, off-dry, or fruit-forward
- **Target grapes:** Gewürztraminer, Riesling, Chenin Blanc, Grenache (fruity red)
- **Principle:** Spice + tannin = pain. Choose aromatic whites or soft fruity reds.
- **Avoid:** Cabernet Sauvignon, Nebbiolo, any high-tannin red

### Chinese (stir-fry, Peking duck, dim sum)
- **Weight:** Varies widely
- **Stir-fry → medium white:** Sauvignon Blanc, Chenin Blanc
- **Peking duck → red:** Pinot Noir, Grenache (mirrors plum/hoisin)
- **Dim sum → sparkling or light white:** Champagne, Riesling

---

## Cheese

### Hard Aged Cheese (Gruyère, Comté, aged Cheddar, Parmesan)
- **Weight:** Medium-Heavy (rich, salty, umami)
- **Wine:** Medium-full red or structured white
- **Target grapes (red):** Nebbiolo, Cabernet Sauvignon, Tempranillo
- **Target grapes (white):** Chardonnay (oaked), Chenin Blanc
- **Principle:** Salt + tannin works. Umami + acid works. Aged Parmesan + Barolo is celestial.

### Soft Cheese (Brie, Camembert, Reblochon)
- **Weight:** Medium (creamy, buttery)
- **Wine:** Sparkling or light-medium red
- **Target grapes:** Champagne, Pinot Noir, Chardonnay, Chenin Blanc
- **Principle:** Bubbles cut creaminess. Pinot Noir's earthiness complements bloomy rind.

### Blue Cheese (Roquefort, Gorgonzola, Stilton)
- **Weight:** Heavy (intense, salty)
- **Wine:** Sweet wine (the classic contrast)
- **Target grapes:** Sauternes (Suduiraut, Coutet, D'Yquem, Climens), Moscato d'Asti
- **Principle:** Sweet + salty = one of the greatest pairings in wine. Sauternes + Roquefort is legendary.

### Goat Cheese (chèvre, fresh or aged)
- **Weight:** Light-Medium
- **Wine:** Crisp, mineral white
- **Target grapes:** Sauvignon Blanc, Chasselas, Arneis
- **Regions:** Marlborough, Vaud, Piemonte
- **Principle:** Acid mirrors citrus tang of fresh chèvre. Loire Sauvignon Blanc + chèvre is the archetype.

---

## Vegetarian & Plant-Based

### Grilled Vegetables (eggplant, zucchini, peppers, asparagus)
- **Weight:** Light-Medium
- **Wine:** Medium white or rosé; light red
- **Target grapes:** Rosé, Sauvignon Blanc, Chardonnay, Grenache, Barbera
- **Note on asparagus:** Notoriously difficult — best with Sauvignon Blanc or Silvaner. Avoid oaky wines.

### Mushroom Dishes (risotto, ragout, stuffed)
- **Weight:** Medium-Heavy (umami)
- **Wine:** Earthy red
- **Target grapes:** Pinot Noir, Nebbiolo, Sangiovese, Barbera
- **Regions:** Bourgogne, Piemonte
- **Principle:** Mushroom earthiness and umami bridge directly to Pinot Noir and Nebbiolo's forest-floor notes.

### Rich Plant-Based (lentil stew, bean chilli, eggplant parmesan)
- **Weight:** Medium-Heavy
- **Wine:** Medium red with good acid
- **Target grapes:** Sangiovese, Barbera, Tempranillo, Grenache
- **Principle:** Treat like medium-weight meat dishes. Acid cuts richness of legumes/cheese.

---

## Desserts

### Fruit-Based (tarts, crumbles, poached pear, fruit salad)
- **Weight:** Light-Medium sweet
- **Wine:** Sweet wine at same or higher sweetness
- **Target wines:** Sauternes (Suduiraut, Coutet, Lafaurie-Peyraguey), Moscato d'Asti
- **Principle:** Stone fruit desserts (peach, apricot) are a natural Sauternes match.

### Chocolate
- **Weight:** Heavy sweet
- **Wine:** Extremely difficult to pair
- **Options:** Very sweet dessert wine (D'Yquem if the chocolate is milk/light), or skip wine
- **Avoid:** Dry reds with chocolate (despite the myth, tannin + cocoa tannin = bitter)
- **Best advice:** Serve chocolate separately, enjoy the Sauternes with cheese course instead

### Pastry / Cream Desserts (crème brûlée, tiramisu, panna cotta)
- **Wine:** Light sweet wine with acid
- **Target wines:** Moscato d'Asti, lighter Sauternes
- **Principle:** Cream desserts need acid in the wine to avoid cloying. Keep it light.

### Cheese Course (as dessert)
- See Cheese section above. Blue cheese + Sauternes is the strongest finish to a meal.

---

## Charcuterie & Appetisers

### Charcuterie Board (salami, prosciutto, coppa, pâté)
- **Weight:** Medium (salty, fatty)
- **Wine:** Medium red, rosé, or sparkling
- **Target grapes (red):** Barbera, Sangiovese, Grenache, Pinot Noir
- **Target grapes (rosé/white):** Rosé, Chenin Blanc, Champagne
- **Principle:** Salt + fat needs acid. Variety on the board → versatile wine. Rosé works for everything.

### Foie Gras
- **Weight:** Heavy (extreme richness)
- **Wine:** Sweet Sauternes (THE classic pairing)
- **Target wines:** Château D'Yquem 2023, Suduiraut (2011, 2014, 2022, 2023), Coutet 2008, Climens 2016
- **Principle:** Unctuous richness + honeyed sweetness + acid = one of gastronomy's greatest matches.

---

## BBQ & Grilling

### BBQ Ribs / Pulled Pork (with sauce)
- **Weight:** Heavy, sweet-smoky
- **Wine:** Full, fruity red (not too tannic — BBQ sauce has sugar)
- **Target grapes:** Malbec, Grenache, Primitivo, Petite Syrah, Syrah
- **Regions:** Mendoza, Puglia, Vallée du Rhône
- **Principle:** Smoke → oak-aged wine. Sweet glaze → fruit-forward wine. Bold flavours need bold wine.

### Mixed Grill / South African Braai
- **Weight:** Heavy, varied proteins
- **Wine:** Full red or robust rosé
- **Target grapes:** Cabernet Sauvignon, Syrah/Shiraz, Pinotage (not in cellar), Cinsault
- **Regions:** Western Cape (Glenelly Lady May is perfect here), Mendoza
- **Principle:** Braai = celebration wine. Open something generous and crowd-pleasing.

### Grilled Sausages (Bratwurst, merguez, etc.)
- **Weight:** Medium
- **Wine:** Easy-drinking red or rosé
- **Target grapes:** Merlot, Grenache, Barbera, Cinsault, rosé blends
- **Price tier:** Budget or everyday — match the casual setting
